question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request body that is exactly maxPayloadLogSize*10 bytes of valid JSON with no whitespace, so that StringUtils.deleteWhitespace() must scan all 3000 characters to confirm there is no whitespace (worst-case scan with no early exit), then allocate a ByteArrayOutputStream and GZIPOutputStream, compress the full 3000 bytes, and Base64-encode the result, all on the hot path of every request?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getRequestTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract call targeting a non-existent contract address, causing the system to execute multiple DB lookups (entity lookup, contract lookup, state lookup) each checked by statementInspector(), and since each lookup is a separate Hibernate statement, the attacker can maximize the number of timeout checks and DB round-trips per request, increasing DB connection hold time and CPU overhead?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request body where the content after whitespace removal is a string of exactly maxPayloadLogSize characters (300 chars), so that the GZIP compression branch is NOT triggered (content.length() == maxPayloadLogSize, not strictly greater), but the content is still logged in full, and by crafting the payload to be exactly at the boundary the attacker avoids compression overhead while still maximizing the logged string size per request?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that LoggingFilter.getContent() calls wrapper.getContentAsString() which internally creates a new String from the cached byte array on every invocation, and since getContent() is called once per request in logRequest(), a 3000-byte buffer means a new 3000-character String is allocated per request, contributing to sustained heap allocation pressure?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getRequestTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract call with a valid but computationally expensive EVM bytecode sequence (e.g., SHA3 opcode in a tight loop within the gas limit), causing the EVM to consume CPU for nearly the full requestTimeout before the next SQL statement triggers the statementInspector() timeout check, effectively allowing up to 10 seconds of CPU-intensive EVM execution per request?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request body containing a JSON string where the 'data' field value is a hex string of exactly 2700 characters (filling most of the 3000-byte buffer), causing the GZIPOutputStream to compress a near-maximum-size payload, and since hex strings have low entropy but are not maximally compressible, the compression ratio is poor, causing the ByteArrayOutputStream to grow beyond its initial capacity (2700/4 = 675 bytes) multiple times?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getRequestTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a contract call that triggers a historical block lookup (block=earliest or a specific block number), causing the system to execute a complex JOIN query against the record_file table, and since the statementInspector() only interrupts at statement boundaries, a single slow historical query can consume the full requestTimeout (10 seconds) of DB connection time, blocking the connection pool for other requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request body where the JSON contains a 'data' field with a value that is a string of alternating commas and closing braces (e.g., ',}' repeated), crafted to maximize the number of positions the DATA_PATTERN regex engine must try for the (}[^}]*)$ anchor, causing the regex to scan the entire string multiple times and increasing per-request CPU consumption?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the GZIPOutputStream in LoggingFilter.getContent() is created with default compression level (Deflater.DEFAULT_COMPRESSION), which is CPU-intensive, and by sending payloads of exactly maxPayloadLogSize+1 characters of highly compressible data (e.g., repeated characters), force the GZIP compressor to run at maximum effort on every request, increasing CPU utilization by 30%+?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getRequestTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract call where the EVM execution triggers many SSTORE or SLOAD opcodes that each require a separate Hibernate SQL query, causing the statementInspector() to be invoked dozens of times per request, each time calling System.currentTimeMillis() and ContractCallContext.get().getStartTime(), adding measurable overhead to every EVM storage operation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getMaxPayloadLogSize()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request body of maxPayloadLogSize*10 bytes where the content is a JSON string with a 'data' field containing a value with many embedded commas and closing braces, causing the DATA_PATTERN regex to match at multiple possible positions and the replaceFirst() call to perform extensive string manipulation, increasing per-request string allocation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getRequestTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the statementInspector() lambda captures the timeout value at bean creation time and never re-reads it from Web3Properties, meaning that if the application is started with a very large requestTimeout (e.g., 1 hour, since there is no @DurationMax constraint), all requests are allowed to hold DB connections for up to 1 hour, enabling a single request to exhaust the DB connection pool?
